content.text: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is a cloud-native identity and access management service. It shares its name lineage with Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) but is architecturally distinct — understanding the differences prevents costly design mistakes when building Azure solutions.

attrs.alt: Active Directory vs Microsoft Entra ID feature comparison

content.text: Active Directory Domain Services was released with Windows 2000 to manage identities and policies for Windows-domain-joined computers on a corporate LAN. It uses X.500-based hierarchical namespaces (forests, domains, organizational units), and authenticates using Kerberos tickets and NTLM — protocols designed for trusted network segments, not the public internet.

Microsoft Entra ID was built from the ground up for the cloud era. It exposes identities over HTTPS using OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML 2.0 — the open standards that every SaaS application, mobile client, and REST API already speaks. The directory is intentionally flat: no forests, no domains, no OUs.
Rule of thumb: AD DS manages machines on a network; Entra ID manages identities on the internet.
The table below compares the key structural concepts side by side:

The protocol difference is the most operationally significant. Kerberos requires a live connection to a domain controller at every logon — a constraint that breaks remote workers, mobile devices, and SaaS apps. Entra ID tokens are self-contained JWTs validated by a public key, so any resource can verify a token without calling home.

1. Client → DC: AS-REQ (Authentication Service Request)
   ← DC returns: TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket), encrypted with krbtgt secret

2. Client → DC: TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Service Request)
   ← DC returns: service ticket, encrypted with service account secret

3. Client → Server: AP-REQ (Application Request with service ticket)
   ← Server decrypts ticket, confirms identity → grants access

Requirement: Client must reach a DC on TCP/UDP 88 (Kerberos) at steps 1–2.
Remote / internet access: requires VPN or DirectAccess to corporate DC.

# Authorization Code Flow (web app) — no DC needed
import msal

app = msal.ConfidentialClientApplication(
    client_id="<app-client-id>",
    client_credential="<client-secret>",
    authority="https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>"
)

# Step 1: redirect user to this URL for interactive login
auth_url = app.get_authorization_request_url(
    scopes=["https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"],
    redirect_uri="https://myapp.example.com/callback"
)

# Step 2: exchange authorization code for tokens
result = app.acquire_token_by_authorization_code(
    code=request.args["code"],
    scopes=["https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"],
    redirect_uri="https://myapp.example.com/callback"
)

access_token = result["access_token"]   # JWT, valid 60–90 min
id_token     = result["id_token"]       # contains user claims (email, name, oid)

The access token is a signed JWT. Any resource that has the Entra ID public key can validate it without a network call to Entra ID — enabling offline validation, edge nodes, and microservices to accept tokens without a dependency on the identity provider at request time.
AD DS stores objects in a hierarchical directory (LDAP): User, Computer, Group, OrganizationalUnit, GroupPolicyObject. Entra ID stores a richer set of objects suited to cloud applications:
User — human identity with email address as UPN; optionally synced from AD DS
Group — security group or Microsoft 365 group; dynamic membership via rules
Application (App Registration) — represents the application's identity contract: redirect URIs, scopes, certificates
Service Principal — the instance of an application registration inside a specific tenant; holds role assignments
Managed Identity — special service principal auto-provisioned for Azure resources (VMs, Functions, AKS); no credentials to manage
Device — Entra-joined or registered device; used for Conditional Access device compliance checks
Service Principals vs AD Service Accounts

# AD DS: traditional service account
New-ADUser -Name "svc-myapp" -UserPrincipalName "svc-myapp@corp.local" `
    -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd!" -AsPlainText -Force) `
    -Enabled $true

# Problems: password rotation, Kerberos SPN registration, account lockout

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Entra ID: service principal with client credentials (no password to rotate)
az ad sp create-for-rbac     --name "sp-myapp-prod"     --role "Storage Blob Data Reader"     --scopes "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystg"

# Output: appId (client_id), password (client_secret), tenant
# Prefer certificate credentials or Managed Identity in production
Hybrid Identity: Connecting AD DS to Entra ID

Most enterprises run a hybrid model: employees already exist in AD DS and need seamless sign-on to cloud services. Microsoft Entra Connect (formerly Azure AD Connect) synchronises objects from AD DS to Entra ID without replacing either system.

On-Premises                          Cloud
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Active Directory DS ──sync──▶ Microsoft Entra Connect ──▶ Microsoft Entra ID
   (users, groups)              (runs on a Windows Server)    (cloud directory)
                                        │
                                   Password Hash Sync  (most common)
                                   Pass-Through Auth   (auth stays on-prem)
                                   AD FS Federation    (full token issuance on-prem)

# Install and configure Microsoft Entra Connect
# Run on a domain-joined Windows Server 2016+

# Download installer: https://www.microsoft.com/download/details.aspx?id=47594

# Post-install: check sync status
Import-Module ADSync
Get-ADSyncConnector | Select-Object Name, Type

# Force a delta sync cycle (normally every 30 minutes)
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta

# Force a full sync (slower, re-reads all objects)
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial

# Check sync errors
Get-ADSyncCSObject -ConnectorName "yourdomain.com" -DistinguishedName "CN=John Smith,OU=Users,DC=corp,DC=local"

# Review sync error report in the Entra admin center:
# https://entra.microsoft.com/ → Identity → Monitoring → Provisioning logs

Password Hash Synchronisation is the recommended mode: a one-way hash of user passwords syncs to Entra ID every 2 minutes, enabling cloud authentication without sending plaintext passwords or requiring on-premises infrastructure availability for every login.

AD DS Group Policy Objects (GPOs) apply machine and user settings — wallpaper, firewall rules, software deployment — to domain-joined Windows computers. They operate at boot and logon over the intranet and require SYSVOL replication between domain controllers.
Entra ID Conditional Access policies evaluate access requests at authentication time using a rich set of signals and apply decisions in real time to any device, anywhere:
Any device, any OS, any location
User risk, sign-in risk, device compliance, app, location, MFA status
Allow, Block, Require MFA, Require compliant device, Limit session
Entra admin center / Microsoft Graph API
Sample Conditional Access Policy via Microsoft Graph
Domain-joined Windows PCs in a corporate office
AD DS (with Entra Connect for hybrid SSO)
AD DS (or Entra Domain Services for lift-and-shift)
B2B partner access to your Azure resources
Customer identity for a public app
Entra External ID B2C (or Entra External Identities)
Entra ID device registration + certificate auth

For lift-and-shift scenarios where legacy apps require Kerberos, LDAP, or NTLM but you don't want to run domain controllers, Microsoft Entra Domain Services (formerly Azure AD DS) provides a managed AD DS-compatible domain in Azure. It syncs users from Entra ID and exposes real LDAP/Kerberos endpoints — no domain controllers to patch.

# Enable Entra Domain Services (portal or CLI)
az ds list --query "[].{Name:name, Location:location, Sku:sku}" -o table

# After provisioning, join an Azure VM to the managed domain
# (same as joining a regular AD domain — set DNS to managed domain IPs)
netdom join <ComputerName> /Domain:<managed-domain> /UserD:<adminUser> /PasswordD:*

# Verify LDAP connectivity from the VM
ldp.exe   # Windows GUI LDAP client
# or
ldapsearch -H ldap://<domain-ip> -D "CN=admin,OU=AADDC Users,DC=aaddscontoso,DC=com" -W -b "DC=aaddscontoso,DC=com" "(objectClass=user)"

Entra Domain Services is one-way: objects sync from Entra ID down into the managed domain, not the reverse. Do not use it as the source of truth for identity — Entra ID remains the authoritative directory.
Managed Identity: Eliminating Service Account Credentials

The most impactful Entra ID capability for cloud developers is the Managed Identity — a service principal automatically provisioned and rotated by Azure for a resource (VM, Function App, AKS pod, Container App). No client secrets, no certificate files, no password rotations.

# Authenticate using a VM's System-Assigned Managed Identity
# No credentials in code — Azure injects a local metadata endpoint

from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()  # picks up MI from IMDS endpoint

# Access Key Vault
kv_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url="https://myvault.vault.azure.net",
    credential=credential
)
secret = kv_client.get_secret("db-connection-string")
print(secret.value)

# Access Storage
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url="https://mystg.blob.core.windows.net",
    credential=credential
)
containers = list(blob_client.list_containers())
print([c["name"] for c in containers])

# Under the hood: ManagedIdentityCredential calls
# http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?resource=...
# Azure injects this endpoint on every MI-enabled resource

# Grant the VM's managed identity access to a storage account
VM_PRINCIPAL=$(az vm show -g rg-prod -n vm-app --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create     --assignee "$VM_PRINCIPAL"     --role "Storage Blob Data Reader"     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystg"

# No secrets created, no rotation needed, revocable instantly

Entra ID access tokens are RS256-signed JWTs. Any service that trusts Entra ID can validate them locally by fetching the tenant's JWKS endpoint — no round-trip to the identity provider per request. This contrasts sharply with Kerberos, where every service ticket must be decrypted with a shared secret stored on the domain controller.
Decoding an Entra ID Access Token

import base64, json, requests

def decode_jwt_payload(token: str) -> dict:
    # JWT: header.payload.signature (base64url encoded)
    parts = token.split(".")
    payload_b64 = parts[1] + "=="  # pad for standard base64
    payload = base64.urlsafe_b64decode(payload_b64)
    return json.loads(payload)

claims = decode_jwt_payload(access_token)
print("Issuer  :", claims["iss"])           # https://sts.windows.net/<tenant-id>/
print("Audience:", claims["aud"])           # app ID URI or resource
print("Subject :", claims["sub"])           # unique object ID for this user+app pair
print("OID     :", claims["oid"])           # user's stable object ID in the tenant
print("UPN     :", claims.get("upn",""))    # user principal name (email)
print("Roles   :", claims.get("roles",[]))  # app roles assigned to this principal
print("Scopes  :", claims.get("scp",""))    # delegated scopes (user context)
print("Expires :", claims["exp"])           # Unix timestamp — typically now + 3600s
Validating Tokens Against the JWKS Endpoint

from jose import jwt as jose_jwt
import requests

TENANT_ID = "<tenant-id>"

# Fetch signing keys (cache these — they rotate infrequently)
oidc_config = requests.get(
    f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration"
).json()
jwks_uri = oidc_config["jwks_uri"]
jwks = requests.get(jwks_uri).json()

# Validate token (verifies signature, expiry, audience, issuer)
claims = jose_jwt.decode(
    access_token,
    jwks,
    algorithms=["RS256"],
    audience="<your-app-client-id>",
    issuer=f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/v2.0"
)

# If jose_jwt.decode() doesn't raise, the token is valid
print("Token is valid. User OID:", claims["oid"])

Libraries like microsoft-identity-web (.NET), MSAL (Python/JS), and passport-azure-ad (Node.js) handle this validation automatically. In production, cache the JWKS response with a TTL of at least one hour to avoid hammering the endpoint on every request.

In AD DS, applications were registered as Service Principal Names (SPNs) on user or computer accounts. In Entra ID, application identity is a first-class object with two parts: the App Registration (the definition) and the Service Principal (the instance in each tenant).
Declares client ID, redirect URIs, API scopes the app exposes
Holds role assignments, consent grants, credential records
Proves the app's identity when requesting tokens
What the app can do on behalf of a signed-in user
What the app can do without a user (daemon/service)
Creating an App Registration and Granting Permissions

# Create an app registration
az ad app create     --display-name "my-api-app"     --sign-in-audience AzureADMyOrg   # single-tenant

APP_ID=$(az ad app list --display-name my-api-app --query "[0].appId" -o tsv)
echo "App (client) ID: $APP_ID"

# Create a service principal for the app in this tenant
az ad sp create --id "$APP_ID"

SP_ID=$(az ad sp show --id "$APP_ID" --query id -o tsv)
echo "Service Principal Object ID: $SP_ID"

# Add a client secret (prefer certificate in production)
az ad app credential reset     --id "$APP_ID"     --append     --years 1     --display-name "prod-secret"

# Assign an Azure RBAC role to the service principal
az role assignment create     --assignee "$SP_ID"     --role "Reader"     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
Declaring API Permissions in a manifest
Security Posture: AD DS vs Entra ID Attack Surface
Knowing the attack surface of each system helps you make informed architectural decisions and avoid hybrid-identity pitfalls.
Token replay, phishing (mitigated by CAE, FIDO2)
SIEM must collect DC event logs (4624, 4672, 4768)
Entra ID Sign-in logs + Audit logs native in Azure Monitor
Enabling Entra ID Sign-In Log Export

# Stream Entra ID sign-in logs to a Log Analytics workspace
TENANT_ID="<tenant-id>"
LAW_ID="/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-security/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-security"

az monitor diagnostic-settings create     --name "entra-signin-logs"     --resource "/tenants/$TENANT_ID/providers/microsoft.aadiam"     --workspace "$LAW_ID"     --logs '[
        {"category": "SignInLogs",        "enabled": true},
        {"category": "AuditLogs",         "enabled": true},
        {"category": "RiskyUsers",        "enabled": true},
        {"category": "UserRiskEvents",    "enabled": true}
    ]'

# Query risky sign-ins in Log Analytics
# SigninLogs
# | where ResultType != 0
# | where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ("high","medium")
# | project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location, RiskLevelDuringSignIn
Most organisations move from pure AD DS toward Entra ID incrementally. These are the most common staged migration patterns:
Phase 1 — Hybrid sync: Deploy Entra Connect with Password Hash Sync. Users get SSO to Microsoft 365 and Azure without changing their AD DS password. Zero downtime.
Phase 2 — Conditional Access: Apply Entra ID CA policies over synced identities. Require MFA for all cloud app access. Block legacy authentication protocols (IMAP, POP3, basic auth).
Phase 3 — Device management: Entra-join new Windows 11 machines. Retire AD DS domain join for new hardware. Use Intune policies instead of GPO for cloud-only machines.
Phase 4 — Application migration: Migrate Kerberos apps to modern auth (MSAL) or proxy them via Entra Application Proxy. Decommission ADFS once all apps use OIDC/SAML.
Phase 5 — Cloud-only identities: For greenfield subsidiaries or new employees, provision directly in Entra ID. No AD DS object required.
Blocking Legacy Authentication with a CA Policy

import requests

# Legacy auth is the #1 attack vector against cloud identities
# (it bypasses MFA entirely — no browser redirect, just username/password)

policy = {
    "displayName": "Block Legacy Authentication",
    "state": "enabled",
    "conditions": {
        "users": {"includeUsers": ["All"]},
        "applications": {"includeApplications": ["All"]},
        "clientAppTypes": [
            "exchangeActiveSync",   # EAS clients (old Outlook)
            "other"                 # IMAP, POP, SMTP AUTH, basic auth
        ]
    },
    "grantControls": {
        "operator": "OR",
        "builtInControls": ["block"]
    }
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/policies",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=policy
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("displayName"))
# Microsoft recommends running in report-only mode first to assess impact:
# change "state": "enabledForReportingButNotEnforced" for 30-day baseline
Graph API or dynamic group rule
Entra ID Smart Lockout (automatic), no manual unlock for cloud accounts
Windows Event Log on each DC (ID 4740, 4720)
Graph API batch requests or HR-driven provisioning (Workday/SuccessFactors)
Bulk User Query via Microsoft Graph

import requests

def list_users(token: str, filter_expr: str = None) -> list:
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    url = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users"
    params = {
        "$select": "displayName,userPrincipalName,accountEnabled,createdDateTime",
        "$top": 999,
    }
    if filter_expr:
        params["$filter"] = filter_expr

    users = []
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        users.extend(data.get("value", []))
        url = data.get("@odata.nextLink")   # pagination
        params = {}                          # nextLink already includes params
    return users

# List only enabled guest users created in the last 90 days
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
cutoff = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=90)).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

guests = list_users(
    access_token,
    filter_expr=f"userType eq 'Guest' and accountEnabled eq true and createdDateTime ge {cutoff}"
)
print(f"{len(guests)} recent guest accounts")
for u in guests[:5]:
    print(f"  {u['userPrincipalName']:<50} created: {u['createdDateTime'][:10]}")

Active Directory Domain Services and Microsoft Entra ID solve the same problem — who you are and what you can do — but for different environments. AD DS uses hierarchical X.500 namespaces, Kerberos tickets, and Group Policy for corporate LANs. Entra ID uses a flat object model, JWT tokens, and Conditional Access for internet-scale cloud and SaaS scenarios.
AD DS: on-premises, Kerberos/LDAP/NTLM, hierarchical (forest/domain/OU), GPO-driven policy
Entra ID: cloud-native, OAuth 2.0/OIDC/SAML, flat directory, Conditional Access policies
Azure RBAC, Microsoft 365, and Entra ID app registrations require Entra ID — not AD DS
Hybrid environments use Entra Connect to synchronise AD DS users into Entra ID
Entra Domain Services provides Kerberos/LDAP for legacy apps without managing DCs
Managed Identities eliminate service account credentials for Azure resources entirely
